metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Science 8 introduces students to cellular biology, the kinetic molecular theory, energy transfer, and plate tectonics. Students develop foundational scientific inquiry skills including observation, hypothesis formation, controlled experimentation, and evidence-based reasoning. First Peoples perspectives and traditional ecological knowledge are integrated throughout as valid and valuable sources of scientific understanding.
metadata.general_description: An integrated science course covering cellular biology, matter and kinetic molecular theory, energy as particle and wave, and plate tectonics — developing scientific inquiry skills and evidence-based reasoning.

big_ideas.statement: Life processes are performed at the cellular level.
big_ideas.elaboration: All living things are made of cells, and the functions that keep organisms alive — respiration, reproduction, response — are carried out by cells and their components.

big_ideas.statement: The behaviour of matter can be explained by the kinetic molecular theory and atomic theory.
big_ideas.elaboration: All matter is made of particles in constant motion. The kinetic molecular theory explains physical properties and changes of state based on how particles move and interact.

big_ideas.statement: Energy can be transferred as both a particle and a wave.
big_ideas.elaboration: Energy moves through matter and space in different ways — as electromagnetic radiation (light), sound, or thermal energy — and understanding these transfers helps explain how the world around us works.

big_ideas.statement: The theory of plate tectonics is the unifying theory that explains Earth's geological processes.
big_ideas.elaboration: The movement of Earth's tectonic plates explains earthquakes, volcanoes, mountain building, and the distribution of continents — a unifying framework for understanding our dynamic planet.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate sustained intellectual curiosity about a scientific topic or problem of personal interest.
i_can_statements: I can identify a scientific question that genuinely interests me and pursue it with curiosity.
I can make careful observations of the natural world and generate questions based on what I notice.
I can formulate a hypothesis and predict what I think will happen in an investigation.

competencies.raw: Collaboratively and individually plan investigations, measure and control variables, and use appropriate SI units.
i_can_statements: I can plan a fair test by identifying and controlling variables.
I can choose the right tools and methods to collect accurate and reliable data.
I can use appropriate SI units and convert between units when needed.
I can follow safety and ethical guidelines during investigations.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Construct and use methods to represent data; seek patterns and connections; apply First Peoples perspectives as information sources.
i_can_statements: I can organize data into tables and graphs to reveal patterns.
I can analyze my data to identify trends, patterns, and relationships.
I can apply First Peoples knowledge and perspectives as valid sources of scientific understanding.
I can use scientific understanding to draw conclusions supported by evidence.

competencies.raw: Reflect on investigation methods; identify sources of error; consider social, ethical, and environmental implications.
i_can_statements: I can identify sources of error in my investigation and suggest specific improvements.
I can evaluate the quality of my data and explain how it could be made more reliable.
I can consider the social, ethical, and environmental implications of a scientific issue.
I can exercise informed skepticism — questioning claims and demanding evidence.

competencies.raw: Contribute to care for self, others, community, and world; transfer learning to new situations; generate ideas when problem-solving.
i_can_statements: I can apply what I've learned in science to a new situation or problem.
I can design a project or solution that addresses a real need in my community or the world.
I can generate and refine creative ideas when problem-solving.

competencies.raw: Communicate findings using scientific language and digital technologies; express varied experiences and perspectives of place.
i_can_statements: I can communicate my scientific findings clearly using appropriate scientific vocabulary.
I can use digital tools to represent and share scientific information.
I can express how my local environment and First Peoples perspectives relate to the science I'm learning.

areas.name: Cells and Life Processes
areas.raw: Life processes performed at the cellular level.
topics: Cell theory: all living things are made of cells; cells come from cells
Cell structure: cell membrane, cytoplasm, nucleus, mitochondria, chloroplasts, cell wall, vacuole
Prokaryotic vs. eukaryotic cells
Unicellular vs. multicellular organisms
Cell processes: diffusion, osmosis, and cell transport
Photosynthesis: light energy converted to chemical energy in plant cells
Cellular respiration: cells release energy from glucose
Cell specialization: how cells become different types in multicellular organisms

areas.name: Matter and Kinetic Molecular Theory
areas.raw: The behaviour of matter explained by the kinetic molecular theory and atomic theory.
topics: Kinetic molecular theory: all matter is made of particles in constant motion
States of matter: solids, liquids, gases — explained by particle movement and energy
Changes of state: melting, freezing, evaporation, condensation, sublimation
Atomic structure: protons, neutrons, electrons; nucleus; electron shells
Elements, compounds, and mixtures
The periodic table: organization by atomic number and properties
Physical and chemical properties of matter
Physical and chemical changes

areas.name: Energy Transfer
areas.raw: Energy can be transferred as both a particle and a wave.
topics: Forms of energy: thermal, light, sound, electrical, kinetic, potential
Energy transfer: conduction, convection, radiation
The electromagnetic spectrum: radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, UV, X-rays, gamma rays
Light as a wave: reflection, refraction, absorption
Sound as a longitudinal wave: compression and rarefaction
Properties of waves: amplitude, wavelength, frequency, speed
Wave-particle duality: an introduction
Practical applications: lenses, mirrors, communication technologies

areas.name: Plate Tectonics
areas.raw: The theory of plate tectonics as the unifying theory of Earth's geological processes.
topics: Evidence for plate tectonics: seafloor spreading, fossil distribution, continental fit
Types of plate boundaries: convergent, divergent, transform
Earthquakes: causes, measurement (Richter scale, moment magnitude), seismic waves
Volcanoes: types (shield, composite, cinder cone), eruptions, and distribution
Mountain building: folding and faulting at convergent boundaries
BC's geological setting: the Cascadia Subduction Zone
First Peoples knowledge of geological phenomena and landforms
Rock cycle: igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks
